summary:
Buddhism arrived in Japan (traditionally dated to 552 AD, with another tradition giving 538 AD) when the [[event:2091|Baekje]] king sent Buddhist texts, an image of the Buddha, and a letter recommending the religion to the [[event:2089|Yamato]] emperor Kinmei. The arrival triggered a political struggle at the Yamato court between the pro-Buddhism Soga clan and the anti-Buddhism Mononobe and Nakatomi clans, who feared that adopting a foreign god would offend Japan's native kami. The Soga prevailed, and Buddhism became the state religion under Prince Shotoku (r. 593–622 AD), who used it as a vehicle for sweeping administrative reforms modeled on Chinese governance.

Prince Shotoku's promotion of Buddhism was transformative for Japanese civilization. He built the Horyuji temple complex (still the world's oldest surviving wooden buildings), issued Japan's first constitution (the Seventeen-Article Constitution, heavily influenced by Buddhist and Confucian principles), sent official diplomatic missions to the Sui Chinese court for the first time, and patronized Buddhist scholarship. Buddhism did not displace the indigenous Shinto tradition but rather entered into a complex syncretic relationship with it — a 'dual Shinto' (shinbutsu-shugo) tradition in which kami were interpreted as local manifestations of Buddhist deities. This synthesis characterized Japanese religion for over a millennium, until the Meiji government forced an official separation in 1868.
